heading: Use the Feed Downstream Systems Wizard
body:
The Feed Downstream Systems wizard helps you set up a federated database instance that writes data from your Atlas cluster to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket continuously on a schedule.

- Click the Create New Federated Database dropdown.

- Select Feed Downstream Systems.

Defaults to `FederatedDatabaseInstance[n]`. Once your federated database instance is
created, you can't change its name.

- Select an Atlas cluster to use as a data source from the dropdown. By default, Atlas Data Federation adds all collections in this cluster. To use a subset of the data, click Specific Collections, expand the databases, and then select the collections that you want to add to your federated database instance.

  > **Tip:** To filter the databases and collections, enter text into
  > the Specific Collections field. The
  > dialog box displays only databases and collections with names
  > that match your search criteria.

- Click Continue.

You can select an existing AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role that Atlas is
authorized for from the role selection dropdown list or choose
Authorize an AWS IAM Role to authorize a new role.

If you selected an existing role that Atlas is authorized for,
proceed to the next step to list your AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) buckets.

If you are authorizing Atlas for an existing role or are creating
a new role, complete the following steps before proceeding to the
next step:

- From the dropdown, select Authorize an AWS IAM Role to authorize a new role or select an existing role.

- Use the AWS (Amazon Web Services) ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and unique
  External ID in the Role Authorization section to add Atlas to the trust
  relationships of an existing or new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role.

  In the Atlas UI, click one of the following:

  - The Create new AWS IAM role shows how to
    use the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and the unique External ID to add Atlas to the
    trust relationships of a new AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) role. Follow the steps
    in the Atlas UI for creating a new role. To learn more, see
    [Create New Role with the AWS CLI](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access).

    When authorizing a new role, if you quit the workflow:

    - Before validating the role, Atlas will not create the
      federated database instance. You can go to the Atlas Integrations
      page to [authorize](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access) a new role, then start
      the procedure for [deploying a federated database instance](gst-deploy-adf) again when you have
      the AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role ARN (Amazon Resource Name).

    - After validating the role, Atlas will not create the
      federated database instance. However, the role is available in the role selection
      dropdown and can be used to create a federated database instance. You do not need
      to authorize the role again.

  - The Use existing AWS IAM role
    shows how to use the ARN (Amazon Resource Name) and the unique External ID to add
    Atlas to the trust relationships of an existing AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management)
    role. Follow the steps in the Atlas UI for adding Atlas
    to the trust relationship to an existing role. To learn more,
    see [Add Trust Relationships to an Existing Role](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access) .

  > **Important:** If you modify your custom AWS (Amazon Web Services) role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) in the future,
  > ensure that the access policy of the role includes the
  > appropriate access to the S3 (Simple Storage Service) resources for the federated database instance.

  To learn more, see [Set Up Unified AWS Access](atlas/security/set-up-unified-aws-access) and
  [Create a Cloud Provider Access Role](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2).

- Click Validate AWS IAM role.

* Enter the name of your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket.

* Follow the steps in the Atlas UI to assign an access policy to your AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role.

* Click Validate AWS S3 bucket access.

* Click Continue.

Schedule a [trigger](atlas-scheduled-trigger) to continuously copy data from your Atlas cluster into your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket using [$out](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/out) in the Schedule Queries section.

- Specify how often you want to extract data from your Atlas cluster using the Repeat Once By dropdowns.

- *Optional*. Specify whether Atlas Data Federation must re-run the same query if it missed it for any reason. By default, this is disabled to allow Atlas Data Federation to re-run missed queries. Alternatively, to skip missed queries and not catch up, toggle on Skip Catch Up Events.

- *Optional*. Choose the format you want for your data when Atlas Data Federation writes it to your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket. Atlas Data Federation supports [Parquet](https://parquet.apache.org/docs/), [CSV](adf-csv-tsv-data) , BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) , and [MongoDB Extended JSON](manual/reference/mongodb-extended-json).

- Specify the Max File Size to limit how large each file that Atlas Data Federation writes to your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket can be. For example, if you set Max File Size to 100 MB and a query returns 1 GB of data, Atlas Data Federation writes the query to your S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket in 10 files, each 100 MBs.

- Enter the [AWS prefix](http://docs.aws.amazon.comAmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-prefixes.html) for your destination S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket.

- Enter the name of the [indexed](manual/indexes) date field in Date Field and specify the format of its value using the dropdown. Every collection that you want to copy downstream must have an [indexed](manual/indexes) field that stores a timestamp as its value.

- Click Continue.
